one_line_takeaway: A PRISMA systematic review of 100 papers mapping FL variants, security/misconduct threats, applications, and open challenges in healthcare FL; useful as a landscape and threat taxonomy, but contributes no new experiments and every metric it cites belongs to the surveyed primary studies.
key_finding: The review's synthesized takeaway is that FL is a viable privacy-preserving paradigm for multi-institutional healthcare ML, materially reducing raw-data-sharing risks (data leakage, model inversion) via differential privacy and secure aggregation, but that its deployment is blocked by persistent challenges: non-IID data heterogeneity (surveyed studies show large IID-vs-non-IID accuracy gaps, e.g. one cervical-cancer FL study reporting 94.36% IID vs 78.4% non-IID), data/device heterogeneity, communication/computation overhead, data bias/fairness, limited scalability, and poor interpretability. The authors frame model misconduct (deliberate or inadvertent injection of errors/bias) as a distinct healthcare-critical risk category because errors can translate into misdiagnosis.
core_contribution: A PRISMA-guided systematic literature review (not an empirical/experimental paper) of Federated Learning (FL) in healthcare. The authors' own contribution is the survey synthesis itself: (1) a taxonomy of six FL variants (Horizontal, Vertical, Federated Transfer Learning, Federated Domain Adaptation, Multitask FL, Federated Meta-Learning) with mapped healthcare use cases; (2) a catalog of 'model misconduct' phenomena and security threats in healthcare FL (data leakage, model poisoning, model stealing, biased models, incentive misalignment) plus a threat-model/adversarial-goal table (data poisoning, model inversion, membership inference, Byzantine attacks); (3) a structured tour of healthcare applications (COVID-19 detection, breast density, healthcare monitoring, medical imaging, EHR); (4) an enumeration of open challenges (data/device heterogeneity, privacy/security, communication-computation efficiency, non-IID data, data bias, limited scalability, lack of interpretability); and (5) future research directions. All numeric results in the paper are quoted from the surveyed primary studies, not produced by the authors.
threat_model: The paper surveys (does not propose or evaluate) threat models for healthcare FL. Assets: sensitive patient data (images, EHRs, genomics) held locally at institutions, and the shared global model. Adversaries include malicious/compromised participating devices, a compromised central aggregator/server, and eavesdroppers on communication channels. Surveyed attack classes: (a) data poisoning / model poisoning — injecting biased or corrupted local data or manipulated gradients/updates to degrade or bias the global model; (b) model inversion — reconstructing private training records from model outputs/updates; (c) membership inference — determining whether a specific patient's data was in the training set; (d) Byzantine attacks — sending incorrect/malicious updates to disrupt convergence or cause failure; (e) model stealing/extraction — replicating the model via query responses; (f) data leakage during insecure transmission or storage. Surveyed defenses (again quoted from cited work, not evaluated by the authors): differential privacy, secure aggregation, gradient sparsification / pseudo-gradient disguising, model watermarking, homomorphic encryption (SHEFL), and blockchain-integrated FL.
limitations: As a survey it produces no new evidence and does not critically re-test or meta-analyze the cited numbers, so all reported metrics inherit the surveyed studies' conditions and cannot be compared head-to-head. Search was limited to two databases (Scopus, Science Directory), journal/peer-reviewed English-language papers only, 2020-2024, potentially missing conference papers (a major venue for FL/ML) and preprints. Data extraction into CSV was done by a single researcher (M.S. Ali). The paper mixes its own organizing categories with loosely defined terms ('model misconduct') and presents threat models and defenses descriptively without quantitative comparison or a systematic risk assessment. Section numbering in the text is internally inconsistent.

datasets_used: No datasets were used by the authors (survey paper with no experiments)
Table 4 catalogs public medical datasets used by SURVEYED FL studies: MIMIC-III (de-identified EHRs of 40,000+ critical-care patients)
NIH Chest X-ray (chest radiographs labeled for pneumonia, tuberculosis, etc.)
Criteo (ad click-through dataset used as a proxy for mortality prediction)
Other datasets named in surveyed studies: Cleveland heart-disease dataset, NSL-KDD (intrusion detection), COVID-19 imaging/EHR datasets
quantitative_results: Review scope (authors' own): 443 Scopus + 163 Science Directory articles retrieved; narrowed to 163; final 100 papers synthesized
FL publication growth cited: 722 (2020) to 5975 (2023), ~2208 by May 3 2024; Computer Science 47%, Engineering 23%, Mathematics 12%, Medicine 2% of FL publications
[cited, Zhang et al. FedUFO] 66.24% overall accuracy on the COVID-19 dataset in a federated setting
[cited, Dheeba et al. ClassifyIT] 87.16% accuracy on the Cleveland dataset vs 78.80% for a regular deep network; MIST-CC raised it to 81.97%, STIR to 85.54%
[cited, Joynab et al.] cervical-cancer CNN FL test accuracy 94.36% (IID) vs 78.4% (non-IID)
[cited, Truhn et al. SHEFL] 80.32% Dice score vs 81.71% for plain FL on cancer image analysis
[cited, Ahsan et al.] Monkeypox classification: M-VGG16 88% (small dataset), 76-77% (imbalanced); M-ResNet50 89% (multiclass); Adam > SGD
[cited, Pan et al.] adaptive FL EHR risk prediction: AUROC 0.679-0.818 (AKI) and 0.659-0.861 (sepsis) across hospitals, outperforming APPLE and RMTL
[cited, Sun et al.] PSO+AdaBoost IDS on NSL-KDD: 12 selected features, recall 0.97
baselines_compared: The survey itself compares no baselines (no experiments)

Baselines appearing WITHIN surveyed studies: FedUFO vs non-fairness FL; ClassifyIT/IPANN vs a regular deep network; Pan et al. adaptive FL vs APPLE and RMTL; SHEFL vs plain FL; non-IID FL algorithms discussed: FedProx, SCAFFOLD, FedNova, FedAvg
